4.2. Model Benchmarking & Selection 
Model RTF (on 
RPi5) 
Latency 
(ms) Licensing Selection Status 
Piper (Onnx) ~0.05 < 100ms MIT SELECTED 
Coqui TTS (VITS) ~1.20 > 1500ms Mozilla Rejected (Too slow) 
gTTS (Google 
Cloud) N/A > 800ms Proprietary Rejected (Req. 
Internet) 
Espeak-NG 0.02 < 50ms GPL Backup (Low Quality) 
 
Benchmarking Analysis: 
• Why Piper? Our testing (as shown in the provided charts) indicates that Piper

TTS using ONNX runtime provides a near-instantaneous response. An RTF of 
0.05 means it can synthesize 1 second of speech in 50ms. 
• Internet Independence: Many speech datasets/models require cloud 
connectivity. By utilizing Piper, which was trained on the LibriTTS and 
Thorsten datasets, we maintain a fully offline system, ensuring user safety 
even in areas with poor network coverage. 
 
4.3. Speech Preprocessing & Synthesis Pipeline

To ensure the navigation commands are clear and "action-oriented, " we 
implemented a text-normalization and streaming pipeline: 
• Instruction Normalization: The raw text from the 
NavigationDeterministicLogic (e.g., "Obstacle at 0.5m") is passed through a 
regex-based normalizer to ensure units like "m" are spoken as "meters" to 
avoid ambiguity. 
• Phoneme Conversion: Piper converts text to phonemes using the eSpeak-
NG backend, which ensures consistent pronunciation of navigational terms.

• Low-Latency Streaming: Instead of generating a full WAV file and then 
playing it, we utilize chunked audio streaming. The system begins playing the 
first syllable of the instruction while the rest of the sentence is still being 
synthesized, reducing the "perceived latency" to near zero. 
 
4.4. Verification of Licensing and Usage 
• Piper / ONNX: MIT License (Permissive for research). 
• LibriTTS / LJSpeech: Public Domain / CC BY 4.0 (Compatible with project 
requirements).

requirements). 
• eSpeak-NG: GPL v3 (Used as a standalone dependency).
